pattern: Classify each string by whether every character is a digit; a fully-numeric string's value is its integer parse, otherwise its value is its character length, so scan once and keep a running maximum.
hints: For each string, first decide which of the two value rules applies — check whether every character in it is a digit.
A digits-only string's value is the number it spells out (parse it as an integer, which naturally handles leading zeros by making them not count), otherwise its value is simply the string's length.
Compute the value for every string in one pass and track the running maximum; no need to store all the values.

constraints.note: each entry length 1..9, lowercase letters and digits only

edge_inputs.description: mix of alphanumeric, alphabetic, and numeric strings
all-numeric strings with leading zeros, comparing parsed values not lengths
all-zero digit strings of varying length — value is always 0, never the length
shortest possible strings, one letter and one digit
longest possible numeric string (9 nines) versus a longer-looking alphanumeric string
a long alphabetic string beats a short numeric string purely on length, even though the numeric one looks bigger visually
a numeric string whose digit value is smaller than its own length (many leading zeros) must use the numeric value, not the length

stress.description: 100 random strings, each either fully numeric (with possible leading zeros) or alphanumeric, length 1-9
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    import string
    n = max(1, size)
    strs = []
    for _ in range(n):
        length = rng.randint(1, 9)
        if rng.random() < 0.5:
            s = ''.join(rng.choice(string.digits) for _ in range(length))
        else:
            pool = string.ascii_lowercase + string.digits
            s = ''.join(rng.choice(pool) for _ in range(length))
            # ensure at least one letter so it is not accidentally all-digit
            pos = rng.randrange(length)
            s = s[:pos] + rng.choice(string.ascii_lowercase) + s[pos+1:]
        strs.append(s)
    return [strs]
stress.note: size caps at the constraint max of 100 entries
